narrative_blocks.header: She was a leader of New York suffrage
narrative_blocks.body: Lillie Devereux Blake became a prominent voice in the nineteenth-century women's rights movement and a leading figure in New York's suffrage campaign. She combined that activism with a working career as a novelist.

narrative_blocks.header: She wrote novels alongside her activism
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond the lecture hall, Blake published novels and sketches that reached a wide popular audience. Her fiction often carried the reformer's concern for women's independence.

narrative_blocks.header: Her fight opened the way to Barnard
narrative_blocks.body: In 1873 Blake petitioned to open Columbia College to women, citing the charter's dedication to the city's youth. Though rebuffed, her long campaign helped lead to the founding of Barnard College in 1889.

education.school_name: Yale College curriculum (with a private tutor)

narrative_blocks.header: She was a leader of New York suffrage
narrative_blocks.body: Lillie Devereux Blake became a prominent voice in the nineteenth-century women's rights movement and a leading figure in New York's suffrage campaign. She combined that activism with a working career as a novelist.

narrative_blocks.header: She wrote novels alongside her activism
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond the lecture hall, Blake published novels and sketches that reached a wide popular audience. Her fiction often carried the reformer's concern for women's independence.

narrative_blocks.header: Her fight opened the way to Barnard
narrative_blocks.body: In 1873 Blake petitioned to open Columbia College to women, citing the charter's dedication to the city's youth. Though rebuffed, her long campaign helped lead to the founding of Barnard College in 1889.

education.school_name: Yale College curriculum (with a private tutor)

narrative_blocks.header: She chose the art world over activism
narrative_blocks.body: Unlike her suffragist mother and educator sister, Elizabeth Robinson was known not for reform but for the art circles of St. Louis, where her husband John Beverly Robinson led Washington University's school of architecture. The family's intellectual energy resurfaced two generations later in anthropology.

narrative_blocks.header: His funeral banished all religious ceremony
narrative_blocks.body: A rationalist and philosophical anarchist, Robinson asked that his 1923 memorial avoid anything funereal or churchly; friends instead rose one by one to tell why they admired him. The architecture professor's service was held in a guild hall hung with paintings, not in a church.

narrative_blocks.header: He designed buildings and fought building law
narrative_blocks.body: A partner in the New York firm Thayer and Robinson, Robinson went on to oversee the city's school buildings and to write Principles of Architectural Composition. A philosophical anarchist, he argued in print against all building legislation, stirring controversy in the engineering press.

narrative_blocks.header: She married at her sickbed with pneumonia
narrative_blocks.body: Stricken with pneumonia two weeks before her 1911 wedding to the pediatrician William McKim Marriott, Elizabeth Robinson refused to postpone; the ceremony was performed at her bedside by a cousin, with no reception. She recovered to raise a family that included the anthropologist McKim Marriott.

narrative_blocks.header: She led one school for thirty-four years
narrative_blocks.body: Katherine Blake became principal of the girls' department of Public School 6 in 1894 and held the post for thirty-four years. In 1916 the school was renamed for her mother, the suffragist Lillie Devereux Blake.

narrative_blocks.header: She marshaled fifteen thousand teachers for suffrage
narrative_blocks.body: Blake organized New York's teachers behind the vote and each summer from 1911 to 1919 was sent by the national suffrage association to campaign in states weighing the question. She marched at the head of the teachers' contingent in the 1915 New York parade.

narrative_blocks.header: She was the board's first woman treasurer
narrative_blocks.body: Blake became the first female treasurer of the New York Board of Education and rose to the presidency of the National Education Association. She founded the city's first evening high school for women in 1897.

narrative_blocks.header: She carried seven million names against war
narrative_blocks.body: As a leader of the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom, Blake was chief spokesperson for the Disarmament Caravan that brought a petition of nearly seven million signatures to President Hoover in 1931 and to Geneva in 1932. In 1928 she was among nineteen educators chosen to accompany John Dewey to Russia.

narrative_blocks.header: Her activism doubled her famous mother's
narrative_blocks.body: Both Katherine and her widowed mother took the surname Blake when Lillie remarried in 1866, and both became suffragists. Katherine later co-wrote her mother's biography, Champion of Women.

cross_connections.display_label: pioneered American women's education a century earlier

education.school_name: School of Pedagogy, New York University

narrative_blocks.header: His obituaries named him for his wife
narrative_blocks.body: In a rare reversal for the era, Grinfill Blake's 1896 death notices identified him chiefly as the husband of the suffragist Lillie Devereux Blake. The New York merchant and his wife had lived apart for his final thirteen years.

sources.short: Online vital records; The Record LXXI; EH 1909

bio.bio_blurb: Irish-born New Bern merchant and shipowner

narrative_blocks.header: A shipwreck made him a Carolina merchant
narrative_blocks.body: An Irish former British naval officer, John Devereux was wrecked off New Bern and stayed, building a fleet that traded between Boston and Barbados. There he married the heiress Frances Pollock, founding a family that would reach into Connecticut and beyond.

sources.short: Online vital records; The Record LXXI; EH 1909

bio.bio_blurb: New Bern heiress; matriarch of the Devereux family

narrative_blocks.header: Husband described as 'Rothschild' of American business
narrative_blocks.body: Frances Pollock's husband John Devereux — Irish-born, trained for the Catholic priesthood at Saint Omer's monastery in France before rejecting priesthood, served as a British Navy midshipman and then 1st Lieutenant during the American Revolution, then sailed penniless to America after his family rejected him — built an estate of approximately eight hundred thousand dollars combined with her Pollock plantation property. Per the North Carolina Biography Project, 'in business circles his name was similar to that of Rothschild in Europe.' Their sons Thomas and George were both sent to Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited eight plantations and Spanish pieces of eight
narrative_blocks.body: Frances Pollock inherited eight plantations plus 7,500 'milled Spanish pieces of eight' from her father Thomas Pollock III's estate. The Spanish pieces of eight were the silver dollars that circulated as the de facto currency of post-Revolutionary North Carolina before the federal Mint produced enough US coinage. Combined with husband John Devereux's substantial Charleston business holdings, the couple became one of the wealthiest planter-merchant families in the Carolinas.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded First Presbyterian Church, New Bern
narrative_blocks.body: Frances Pollock Devereux co-founded the First Presbyterian Church in New Bern North Carolina, the family's antebellum home. Her daughter Frances Ann Devereux Polk would later marry Bishop Leonidas Polk in 1830 and become the wife of one of the most prominent Episcopal bishops of the antebellum South — carrying the family religious commitment into the next denomination and the next generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Born New Bern 1771, died Raleigh 1849
narrative_blocks.body: Frances Pollock was born March 18, 1771 in New Bern North Carolina, daughter of Thomas Pollock III and Eunice Edwards — making her granddaughter of Jonathan Edwards and great-great-granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker through Sarah Pierpont Edwards. She died June 3, 1849 in Raleigh North Carolina at age seventy-eight and was buried in City Cemetery Raleigh.
